Battle of Asal Uttar

[Indian Army](https://sadashree.substack.com/s/indian-army/?utm_source=substack&utm_medium=menu)

# Battle of Asal Uttar

[](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

![Ratnakar Sadasyula's avatar](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!R73I!,w_36,h_36,c_fill,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F81a2c542-8b3f-4ade-98d2-a40950cffbe3_2640x1980.jpeg)

[Ratnakar Sadasyula](https://substack.com/@sadashree)

Sep 08, 2023

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

2

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Share

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8CNW!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6912a7d5-6a0c-4a38-8ca7-f0e33b339217_297x375.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!8CNW!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F6912a7d5-6a0c-4a38-8ca7-f0e33b339217_297x375.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Havaldar Abdul Hamid who won the Param Vir Chakra, for his heroics in Battle of Asal Uttar, was born in Ghazipur district of UP. He joined the Indian Army in December 1954 and was posted to the 4th Battalion of the Grenadiers Regiment. During the 1962 Sino-Indian War, his battalion was part of the 7th Infantry Brigade under Brigadier John Dalvi. At Namka Chu, surrounded and cut off by Chinese forces, the battalion made a daring breakout into Bhutan on foot—a retreat that became a symbol of grit under impossible odds.

The M40 recoilless rifle was no ordinary weapon—it was a 105 mm crew-served anti-tank gun, deceptively simple in design but devastating in the right hands. And Abdul Hamid wielded it like a master.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!k5el!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5a531ae5-83ba-4c8a-b7f9-3f34c33eb1dc_602x412.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!k5el!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5a531ae5-83ba-4c8a-b7f9-3f34c33eb1dc_602x412.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Khem Karan may seem like just another quiet border town, but in September 1965, it became the crucible of one of modern India’s most defining military engagements: the **Battle of Asal Uttar**.

From **8 to 10 September 1965**, the fields around Asal Uttar witnessed the **second-largest tank battle** since WWII. Over **250 Pakistani tanks**, including the formidable M47 and M48 Pattons, clashed with Indian Centurions, Shermans, and AMX-13s.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!QFKJ!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4e79a4fa-9132-41b4-8a81-39c256f74183_299x197.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!QFKJ!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F4e79a4fa-9132-41b4-8a81-39c256f74183_299x197.jpeg)

Pakistan’s invading force consisted of around 200 Patton Tanks, consisting of the 1st Armored Division and 11th Infantry Division primarily, under Maj Gen Nasir Ahmed Khan and Brig A.R.Shami. Named after the World War II hero, the M48 Patton Tanks was one of the most powerful and deadly tanks, known for it’s firepower and assault capabilities. It was used in many of the post World War II conflicts that included the Iran-Iraq War, Yom Kippur War, Vietnam War, 6 Day War among others.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WqRN!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F91fd632f-3076-4fa7-80a6-90a0bb528a5e_300x187.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!WqRN!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F91fd632f-3076-4fa7-80a6-90a0bb528a5e_300x187.jpeg)

India on the other hand had just 135 tanks out of which 45 were French AMX-13 a light tank, that would prove to be critical. Another 45 were the US M4 Sherman tanks, which were primarily medium range and used a lot in the Arab-Israeli wars. And finally the Centurion tanks used for nuclear weapons testing, used a lot by Israel.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!iDs3!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8946cc20-5894-4328-b84d-885c424e0602_300x225.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!iDs3!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F8946cc20-5894-4328-b84d-885c424e0602_300x225.jpeg)

Lt. Gen. Harbaksh Singh was the kind of leader who didn’t just command armies; he inspired them.

Born in **Badrukhan village near Sangrur** in 1913, he was a towering figure both in stature and spirit. His military journey began in the British Indian Army, and by the time WWII erupted, he was already a seasoned officer.

During the **Malayan Campaign**, he was **severely wounded** and later taken as a **prisoner of war in Singapore** in 1942. He endured years of captivity under the Japanese, suffering from typhoid and beriberi—ailments that left a lasting mark on his health.

But it was in **1947–48**, during the first Indo-Pak war, that he truly came into his own. As a brigade commander, he led a daring assault over the **Nasta Chun Pass to Tithwal**, reclaiming critical territory in Kashmir. His leadership was swift, decisive, and deeply rooted in personal courage.

Fast forward to **1965**, and he was now the **General Officer Commanding-in-Chief of Western Command**. When Pakistan launched its offensive, many expected a fallback. In fact, the then Army Chief reportedly advised a withdrawal to the Beas River. Harbaksh Singh refused. He held the line at **Asal Uttar**, orchestrated the counteroffensive at **Phillora and Chawinda**, and turned the tide of the war.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!IC5q!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F24959f2d-9b44-45df-be72-61dea4097406_468x350.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!IC5q!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F24959f2d-9b44-45df-be72-61dea4097406_468x350.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

Maj Gen Gurbaksh Singh’s decision to fall back and form a **horseshoe-shaped defensive arc around Asal Uttar** was nothing short of strategic brilliance. It turned the terrain into a trap—flooded fields that bogged down the advancing Pattons, rendering their superior firepower ineffective.

Despite being under assault from three enemy prongs, Gurbaksh Singh didn’t just issue orders from a safe distance—he was **on the ground**, rallying his troops, reinforcing positions, and ensuring that morale never wavered. His leadership was instrumental in transforming what could have been a rout into a resounding stand.

Awarded the **Maha Vir Chakra**, his citation reflects not just tactical acumen but also the emotional and psychological strength to hold a fragile line under immense pressure

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lEkh!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F978633ab-9a59-42bc-8f09-7f6e810a1fef_209x300.png)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!lEkh!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F978633ab-9a59-42bc-8f09-7f6e810a1fef_209x300.png)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The battle began on September 8 with the Pakistani tanks launching their attack, moving within 900 miles of Indian defenses.

The **Deccan Horse (9th Horse)**, a storied cavalry regiment, was positioned in the thick sugarcane fields near Asal Uttar. These fields weren’t just cover—they were camouflage crafted by nature. Indian tanks lay in wait, engines off, crews silent, watching through narrow slits as the Pakistani Pattons advanced confidently into what they thought was open ground.

As the Pakistani spearhead moved within striking range—just **900 meters** from Indian lines—the trap was sprung. The Deccan Horse opened fire with deadly precision. In that initial clash alone, **11 Patton tanks were destroyed**, while India lost only **4 tanks**. The psychological impact was immense: the myth of Patton invincibility was shattered in a single, smoky salvo.

September 9 was the crucible day of the Battle of Asal Uttar, when resolve was tested in fire and steel.

At **2 AM**, Pakistani forces launched a **night attack** on **18 Rajputana Rifles**, leveraging **infrared night-vision equipment**—a technological edge that gave them confidence in the dark. But the defenders, under **Brigadier Hanut Singh**, held firm. The Pakistani tanks were funneled into **minefields**, then pounded by **five Indian artillery regiments**, creating a deadly gauntlet.

When communication lines were severed and forward companies isolated, **Lt Col Raghuvir Singh** did the unthinkable.

He **moved past three enemy tanks** under heavy fire, reached the embattled companies, **re-established contact**, rallying his men. His leadership inspired the destruction of **20 enemy tanks**, and he was posthumously awarded the **Maha Vir Chakra.**

All day, **Lt Col Bhatti’s 4 Grenadiers** faced relentless attacks. Despite exhaustion and mounting pressure, they repelled wave after wave, aided by the terrain and sheer grit.

The **Deccan Horse** launched a **furious counterattack**, using the sugarcane fields for cover. Their Shermans, though technically inferior, were positioned with surgical precision. Pakistani tanks were knocked out in droves, and by **10 PM**, the enemy was forced to **retreat**, leaving behind wreckage and wounded pride.

**September 10**

Pakistan’s **4th Armoured Brigade**, bolstered by elements of the **1st Armoured Division**, launched a massive offensive aimed at **enveloping the Bhura–Karimpur–Mahmudpur axis**. Their goal: to break through the Indian defenses, isolate the 4th Mountain Division, and open the road to Amritsar.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xDSz!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5062a2ca-fc72-4034-b80f-de5b9d780c7e_481x546.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!xDSz!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2F5062a2ca-fc72-4034-b80f-de5b9d780c7e_481x546.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

The counter strategy here was the brainchild of Brig General Thomas Teograj, who was in charge of the 2nd Independent Armored Brigade.

[](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!YueF!,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Facce6b61-e19e-4c1b-be13-a8d60b3deee9_450x253.jpeg)

![](https://substackcdn.com/image/fetch/$s_!YueF!,w_1456,c_limit,f_auto,q_auto:good,fl_progressive:steep/https%3A%2F%2Fsubstack-post-media.s3.amazonaws.com%2Fpublic%2Fimages%2Facce6b61-e19e-4c1b-be13-a8d60b3deee9_450x253.jpeg)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

![](data:image/svg+xml;base64,)

When Pakistan’s 1st Armoured Division surged into the Khem Karan sector on September 8, 1965, Theogaraj’s brigade was tasked with halting their advance.

He orchestrated the horseshoe-shaped defensive trap around Asal Uttar. Ordered the **flooding of sugarcane fields**, turning the terrain into a quagmire that immobilized the Patton tanks. Positioned Indian armor—Centurions, AMX-13s, and Shermans—**in concealed ambushes**, using the cane fields as natural camouflage.

The result? Over **100 Pakistani tanks destroyed or abandoned**, many of them Pattons. The battlefield became a graveyard of steel, and Theogaraj’s brigade stood as the wall that broke Pakistan’s armored spearhead.

For his strategic brilliance and battlefield composure, Brigadier Theogaraj was awarded the **Maha Vir Chakra**. He later wore the badges of the Pakistani regiments he had defeated—**4th Cavalry, 6th Lancers, 5th Horse, and 24th Cavalry**—as a quiet, defiant tribute.

On **September 10, 1965**, as Pakistani tanks breached Indian defenses near **Chima village**, **Company Quartermaster Havildar Abdul Hamid** once again took to his **jeep-mounted M40 recoilless rifle**. Under a hail of enemy fire, he **destroyed three Patton tanks** in rapid succession, including the lead tank that was spearheading the assault.

Eyewitness accounts describe how he would **wait in silence**, let the tanks come close, and then strike with surgical precision. His actions not only stalled the enemy’s advance but **inspired his comrades to hold the line**. Some sources credit him with destroying **up to 8 tanks** over two days—a feat unmatched in the annals of Indian warfare.

But as he maneuvered to engage a fourth, a direct hit from enemy tank fire struck his position. Hamid was **mortally wounded**, yet his final stand ignited something fierce in his comrades. Inspired by his sacrifice, the Indian troops launched a **furious counterattack**, pushing back the Pakistani armor and sealing the fate of the battle.

His actions that day weren’t just tactical—they were **transformational**. He turned fear into fury, retreat into resistance. Posthumously awarded the **Param Vir Chakra**, his legacy lives on not just in medals, but in the soil of Punjab, where sugarcane fields once whispered his name.

*Whoever remains cooler under stress for a longer time will win.Identify,take good aim and shoot’.God be with you.’’ -Lt.Col Caleb*

And this is what happened, as the Indian soldiers, spurred on by Hamid’s sacrifice, displayed exemplary courage under fire, and smart tactics to counter the invading Pakistani forces.

In the sugarcane fields near **Dholan**, **Major Sandhu’s Centurions** lay in wait like silent sentinels. When the Pattons rolled in, confident and unaware, they were met with a blistering ambush. **Four Pattons were destroyed**, their hulks left smoldering amid the cane stalks.

At **Madar**, Indian Centurions positioned in the **second arc of the horseshoe formation** opened fire with devastating precision. The terrain, once again, was the great equalizer—concealing movement, amplifying surprise. Several Pattons were knocked out here, further fracturing the enemy’s armored thrust.

**2nd Lt R.P. Joshi**, barely out of training, executed a textbook ambush on the **2nd Pakistani Armoured Squadron** near **Mahmudpura**. His unit destroyed **9 Pattons and 2 recoilless rifles**, a staggering blow that sent shockwaves through the enemy ranks.

As another squadron attempted to regroup and flank, **Naib Risaldar Jagdeo Singh** led a swift counterstrike. **Three Pattons were knocked out**, and the rest of the squadron was routed, their formation broken and morale shattered.

By this point, the battlefield was no longer contested—it was **claimed**. The myth of the Patton’s invincibility lay buried beneath the mud of Punjab, and the Indian soldiers, fueled by sacrifice and strategy, had carved their names into legend

By **September 11, 1965**, the Pakistani offensive had unraveled. Their **infantry was pinned down** by relentless Indian artillery, while the **4th Cavalry and 4th Armoured Brigade**—once the pride of Pakistan’s armored might—were **encircled and decimated**. The battlefield had become a crucible of fire, mud, and shattered ambition.

**Maj Gen Nasir Ahmed Khan**, commander of Pakistan’s 1st Armoured Division, was **mortally wounded** as his formation crumbled around him.

**Brigadier A.R. Shami**, the artillery commander, was **killed in action**, a blow that further disoriented the Pakistani response.

As dusk fell, **Indian artillery unleashed a final barrage**, breaking the last vestiges of resistance and morale.

  
With the chain of command shattered and the battlefield littered with burning Pattons, a **Pakistani regimental commander surrendered** on **September 11**, marking the symbolic end of the Battle of Asal Uttar. It was no longer a contest of arms—it was a reckoning.

It was a massive rout for the Pakistan Army, 97 tanks were destroyed, including 75 Pattons, while India lost just 14. 2 Pakistani armored regiments were wiped out, Asal Uttar lived up to it’s name. The rout forced Pakistan into a defensive strategy, and it never recovered.

The destroyed and captured Patton tanks were assembled at a place called Patton Nagar. Pakistan in spite of having the much superior Patton tanks and night fighting equipment, lost to a combination of smart strategy, coordinated infantry attack and excellent leadership at the top. And above all the sugarcane fields, which provided an excellent hiding cover for the Indian tanks, and also the flooding of canals, which bogged down the Pakistani tanks.

Sources

http://www.bharat-rakshak.com/ARMY/history/1965war/275-war-history-1965.html

https://historyunderyourfeet.wordpress.com/2019/09/09/battle-of-asal-uttar-1965-war/
